scope: Algebra I foundation lesson content correctness
findings: Module 6 objectives are covered end-to-end: solving linear inequalities (slides 06 and 07), graphing solutions on a number line with open/closed endpoint markers (slides 04, 06, 07, 11, 12), and writing solutions in interval notation alongside set-builder notation (slides 10 and 12).

Worked example 3x + 5 < 14 is traced correctly: subtract 5 from both sides to get 3x < 9, then divide both sides by +3 with no flip required, landing on x < 3. Graph shows an open circle at 3 with shading to the left; interval (−∞, 3) uses round brackets on both ends, which is correct because neither −∞ nor 3 is included.

Worked example −2x + 7 ≥ 1 is traced correctly: subtract 7 to get −2x ≥ −6, then divide by −2 AND flip ≥ to ≤, landing on x ≤ 3. Graph shows a closed dot at 3 with shading to the left; interval (−∞, 3] uses a square bracket on the included endpoint 3 and a round bracket on −∞.

Pause #1 (4x − 3 ≥ 17 → x ≥ 5; interval [5, ∞)) and Pause #2 (closed dot at −2, shading right → x ≥ −2; interval [−2, ∞); set-builder { x | x ≥ −2 }) are arithmetically correct, match their answer-reveal slides, and reinforce the rule that infinity always uses a round bracket.

Compare slide 05 correctly characterizes the flip rule by walking the same inequality −2x < 10 through both the wrong (kept-sign) and the right (flipped) paths and uses x = 0 as a test value to show that the un-flipped answer x < −5 excludes a value that satisfies the original inequality.

Interval translation table on slide 10 lists four canonical cases (x < 3, x ≤ 3, x > −2, x ≥ −2) with matching interval and set-builder notation, and the endpoint column reinforces the open-vs-closed bracket meaning.
release_notes: Foundation math only; no AP, College Board, CEEB, accreditation, Common App, NCAA, or admissions claims appear anywhere in the lesson.
